title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure-related items including a state transportation funding resolution, a recycled asphalt crushing contract with King Gravel for $126,900, and construction service agreements for upcoming road projects. The committee also directed staff to develop $100/day penalty language for illegal sewer discharges after discussion of significant clear water infiltration adding millions of gallons to wastewater treatment costs.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Water/sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue duplex
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project may be moved to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered upcoming Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Schofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street restoration work.

Staff reported on road limits removal, spring sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup off East Nick, inlet repairs on Birch Street, and overhead sign installation on Schofield Avenue. Discussion of flooding issues on Weston Avenue area included use of sandbags and dewatering pumps for about a week. Staff is considering permanent pump solutions. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch pipe.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration into sanitary sewers. April data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the water system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers as a major contributor. INI study with in-pipe technologies is planned to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenue of approximately $3 million and net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with 6.7% infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves 6,100 customers total including 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in Schofield/Rib Mountain Metro. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution requests long-term sustainable funding solutions for the state transportation fund, noting that current grant funding helps one-time costs but recurring operational funding remains inadequate. The resolution will be sent to the governor and state legislators. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. This compares favorably to City of Wausau paying $28/cubic yard versus Weston's $17.11/cubic yard, saving approximately $130,000 across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought committee guidance on enforcement of illegal sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers. Current ordinance allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing versus willful violations. After discussion, committee directed staff to develop penalty language starting at $100/day for violations, with motion passing unanimously. Staff will wait for INI study results to identify problem areas before sending violation notices.

Michael presented an agreement allowing a duplex builder on lot one at 2230 Radkkey Avenue to temporarily connect to existing water/sewer mains via long lateral service until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added language requiring reconstruction "no later than 2029" when the owner would pay assessment for permanent main extension. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for construction on Military Road from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for utility relocation (sanitary sewer $6,156 and water main $88,704) totals $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Water main requires relocation due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion to approve passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Service Proposals - Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael presented construction inspection service proposals. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730; staff recommended Clark Dietz for the extra $3,000 due to having full-time experienced staff rather than summer intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Staff to develop $100/day penalty language for illegal sanitary sewer discharges
Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated)
Village Board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding solutions
Village Board to authorize water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline no later than 2029
Village Board to approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelnik project ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel project ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Water main flushing notices going out with flushing to begin next week
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to provide estimate of treatment costs from clear water infiltration at next meeting
Staff to look at including street lighting in Schofield Avenue project from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield has been canceled, potentially boosting attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated by a commissioner and elected as Vice Chair unanimously, with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. The motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill from 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year and announced that the competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole balloon event in the area. A commissioner noted they requested $5,000 but are booking 20 fewer hotel rooms than last year. The commission discussed the need for better room tracking data from hotels. The commission approved a $5,000 grant (same as last year) unanimously, with a request that the organization provide actual hotel room usage data in their post-event report.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting that Anastasia won the competition. The candidates toured Marathon County attractions including Mullen's Cheese. This was noted as the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide actual hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with minimal discussion.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Breath and communication disclosure and recusal
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property, allowing the parking lot to be placed over the pipeline while keeping the building on higher ground to reduce fill costs. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond the current 40 officers. Discussion included an alternate storage shed location and future expansion possibilities to the north. A motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with staff conditions 2 through 5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and site drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. Jennifer presented a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be contacted. Commissioner Lumper praised the program as well put together. Motion to approve passed unanimously with addition that staff report back to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee.
Motion to approve minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email confirmation and calendar invites.

publicComment: Jim Pensson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the new police department to avoid unnecessary taxpayer expense, similar to accommodations made for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to track compliance over time.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted with twice-annual inspections beginning summer 2026
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works committee around May 2027
Staff to contact existing salt storage facility near Sternberg well about wellhead protection compliance
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send comprehensive plan steering committee meeting details via email
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall 2026
Staff to share Ehlers presentation on residential TIF districts with commission

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new members, featuring educational presentations from department heads about the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No formal votes were taken beyond approving minutes, as the meeting focused on introducing committee members to departmental missions, services, and budget structures.

agenda.item: Call to order and pledge of allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04 - missions, duties, responsibilities
Veteran Service Office budget and data discussion

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli moved to approve the April 1st committee meeting minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. Motion carried unanimously with voice vote.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect against environmental hazards. She highlighted 1,200+ licenses overseen, communicable disease monitoring, nurse family partnership program with first graduates, public health vending machines stocked with free tick kits and harm reduction supplies, and a new homeless system response position funded by the county board and City of Wausau partnership.

Administrator Lance Leonard presented on behalf of absent Director Christa, explaining the department's divisions including child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program that addressed housing instability for child welfare and youth justice cases, noting the program substantively changed based on board feedback.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his department grew from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE in 2025. Customer interactions jumped from 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. Veterans in Marathon County received $44 million annually in VA benefits, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. He noted Marathon County had 30 suicides in 2025, seven of which were veterans, prompting focus on suicide prevention grants. The office is pursuing surviving spouse benefits outreach and partnering with Center for Veterans Issues on housing stability.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the four-county regional organization (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) provides aging and disability resource services funded primarily by state/federal sources with 11% county levy. He noted significant financial challenges as nutrition program costs increased 40-45% over four years while federal Older Americans Act funding remained flat since 2024. The ADRC hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024.

actionItems: North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before committee
Health Department to provide location information for public health vending machines
New strategic plan development to be discussed at future meetings with goal of completion by December
Next meeting scheduled for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and held extensive discussion about relocating the farmers market to Kennedy Park due to the previous location being developed. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order and roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances - mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes
Committee appointments and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fee Amendment
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300/$250 to $761 for single family, $563 for duplex, and $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the lack of detailed cost data to justify the fees. Trustee Clark supported the fee increase citing population growth and the need for new residents to contribute to park costs. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule was also approved unanimously with motion by Hermling, second by Clark.

Chief Finkey explained that Safer and surrounding municipalities (Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others) created a reciprocal inspection agreement where one fire inspection sticker is valid across all participating communities, eliminating redundant inspections for food trucks operating in multiple jurisdictions. Ordinance 26-008 was approved unanimously with motion by Lope, second by Zagami.

Significant debate occurred over the policy language. Trustees Beck and Olsen questioned whether the draft policy matched what was previously discussed, noting the policy still contained language about health, safety, and welfare justifications that the board had voted to remove to make the policy broader. The board voted to amend the policy to strike sentences requiring justification reasons, with Hermling and Clark voting no on the amendment. The final amended policy passed with Hermling and Clark voting no, requiring only 12-hour advance notice, camera on, and no participation in closed session.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Extensive discussion-only item about the farmers market's relocation from Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue (where property owner is developing) to Kennedy Park. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass along Alderson Street. Renee explained history: the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors appreciate having vehicles with them for shelter and restocking. Concerns were raised about grass damage and traffic. Trustee Clark noted if no suitable location can be found, the market may need to pause. Renee confirmed the market operates at a profit through vendor fees and grants, not a loss.

Director Dolan explained that two engineering inspection contracts totaling approximately $108,000 are needed for summer projects because the engineering technician position remains unfilled. He noted the specialized skillset is difficult to recruit in central Wisconsin, with even consulting firms losing employees to relocation. The contracts are for projects starting in June.

discussions.item: Secondhand Dealer License for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC
discussions.body: Chief Hunt explained the license is required under state statute for businesses buying and selling precious metals and used items, with merchandise tracked through a police-accessible database for stolen property verification. The motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously with motion by Hermling, second by Clark.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Goldin Mua to the CLIPS committee. Openings remain on Parks, Finance/HR (two), and CLIPS (one). Board agreed that future committee applications should be included in meeting packets for trustee review.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 5702 Street spoke during public comment criticizing the $108,000 in outside engineering contracts, questioning why the engineering technician position remains unfilled, and stating the administrator needs to be held accountable. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before being officially appointed, and complained that the Kennedy Park funding information requested at a prior meeting was not included in the packet. He also questioned the justification for the park impact fee increase during that public hearing.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Ordinance 26-008 approved creating reciprocal mobile food vendor inspection program
Ordinance 26-009 approved amending standing committees section
Ordinance 26-011 approved amending nuisances chapter
Ordinance 26-012 approved amending bicycle regulations for e-bikes
Resolution 2026-014 adopted supporting sustainable state transportation funding
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Goldin Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC pending background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Speed study on Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to board
Comprehensive Plan Steering Committee meeting scheduled for June 1 at 6:00 PM
Future committee applications to be included in board packets
Staff to consider intergovernmental agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspections of tornado-damaged homes

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the at-large Executive Committee positions, and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for storm debris following severe weather that devastated the Ringle area. The board heard emotional public testimony from affected residents requesting fee waivers and discussed potential future relief options through the community foundation.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of meeting notice and roll call
Committee appointments discussion and Chair remarks on leadership
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks thanking Vice Chair Boots for assistance developing appointments, emphasizing that 34% of the board is in their first or second term. He outlined goals including increasing diversity of perspectives, providing leadership development opportunities, and honoring supervisor preferences. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Supervisor Leur, stating she has 'advocated for our county's smallest children and our oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
discussions.body: County Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for storm debris following the April severe weather that devastated Ringle. The standard $65/ton fee would be reduced to approximately $36.94 by waiving state fees ($13.10), town of Ringle fees ($2.33), and reducing to commercial rate. Leonard noted the county will not meet the $670,743 threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle to qualify. He mentioned discussions with the community foundation about helping residents with unreimbursed insurance costs. The board suspended rules unanimously to consider the resolution from the Solid Waste Management Board, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.

At Supervisor Rosenberg's request, the board allowed two residents to speak. Jessica Toasty asked the county to waive fees entirely for six months, noting that businesses have donated over $40,000 in cleanup costs and the state, DNR, and Ringle are all waiving their portions. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, requesting fee waivers for underinsured residents and asking for more than 60 days since many insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty asked the county to waive all fees for six months, citing that the state, DNR, town of Ringle, and private businesses are all contributing while Marathon County has provided limited financial assistance. Matt Haybeck requested fee waivers for underinsured residents facing home demolition and asked for timeline extensions since some insurance adjusters haven't arrived.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large Executive Committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approving out of home placement reserve increase approved (not unanimous)
Resolution 25-26 amending social services budget approved (not unanimous)
Resolution 26-26 reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris through June 17th approved unanimously
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with community foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on disaster-related fee reductions within six months
Staff to track certified storm debris and review town of Ringle resolution for fee waiver scope

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified resolution waiving only reconnect fees (not other zoning or septic fees) for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
Consideration of fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview presentation
Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview presentation

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the town of Green Valley. The town board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
discussions.body: The petition sought to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy designation; Garrett Pogle explained it contained a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make these conforming uses. No natural areas would be lost. The town board recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Miskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. Significant debate occurred about setting precedent and fairness to future disaster victims. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about waving fees while the county recently increased POWTS fees. Supervisor Sefelt moved to forward the resolution; Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160) rather than all fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented on solid waste operations, noting the facility accepts about 1,000 tons daily with 11 FTEs. He highlighted the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties for the county, upcoming leachate treatment requirements by 2032 due to PFAS concerns, and significant capital expenses including $6 million for 11-acre expansion. He noted lithium-ion battery fires occur about once weekly.

Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. The department operates 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest across 10 units, and manages city parks under contract. Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Lovelin announced his retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement.

actionItems: Rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for James Offer and David Tract property in Green Valley forwarded to county board for approval
Rezoning from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property in McMillan forwarded to county board for approval
Modified resolution waiving only POWTS reconnect fees ($160) for tornado-damaged properties forwarded to county board for consideration
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to severe weather fee waivers for future policy development

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved signage for the Plover River Water Trail kayak launches and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month. The meeting featured public comment opposing dogs at the pool and criticism of Kennedy Park fundraising transparency, while staff provided updates on the Kennedy Park project timeline and Yellow Banks disc golf course maintenance.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes from the previous meeting. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

Director Sean Oster Bring presented a timeline document showing the Kennedy Park master plan process from the 2023 approval through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funding and the fundraising timeline. Staff explained the Friends of Kennedy Park nonprofit is responsible for fundraising with a 10-year goal, and the village has paused infrastructure projects pending fundraising progress. The Friends group plans to publicly announce fundraising totals at their June tournament. Staff noted Youth Baseball contributed $8,700 toward field equipment and the group is approximately $17,000 short of the $117,000 needed for upper field lights.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing tree removal locations along the river and installation of wooden mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. Dan from the disc golf group explained the mats are village-owned and some extras may be used on another trail project. The pipeline company (now TC Energy, formerly ATC/ANR) conducted site visits during the work.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the June meeting because Chair Katrina, who requested the agenda item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. Public commenter Andy from 6504 [address unclear] stated opposition, saying 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period. I like dogs, but it's not a place for them. We have rivers. We have lakes.'

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches along the trail, noting the pressure washer cannot access the area due to steep ditches and narrow bridges. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, and potential volunteer workdays. Committee members expressed interest in community involvement for maintenance. No formal action was taken; staff will research options.

Dan Corvino from 2625 Northwestern Avenue presented version 7 of signage for the Plover River Water Trail, explaining signs will be installed at launches 3, 4, and 5 in Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge information, and location-specific history sections. Funding comes from Marathon County Parks Foundation and state grants. A motion to approve installation of the signs passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in exploring food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds after receiving an email request. Committee agreed to bring it back for discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting next Tuesday with Rettler, and the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7pm.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston) spoke online under the name 'Vern M,' criticizing the 4pm meeting time and questioning Kennedy Park fundraising transparency, specifically the $8,700 expenditure figure and asking about the location of the $10,000 grant funds and glow event proceeds.
actionItems: Minutes from April 27, 2026 approved unanimously
Kayak launch signage approved for installation at Weston locations - to be forwarded to Village Board
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to research graffiti removal and protective coating options for Oaklair River Trail
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting scheduled for next Tuesday with Rettler
Food allergy awareness signage to be brought back for future discussion
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged budget reports for April. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office budget, elections administration, licensing, and operations. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving away from Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. No discussion. Motion carried unanimously.

Discussion focused on TIF closure timeline. Staff indicated they are anticipating October-November for final bond payoff, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. Staff confirmed final numbers still look good and the village will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented on the clerk's office structure (2 full-time, 1 part-time employee, down from 4 full-time previously), elections administration including Badger Book electronic poll books ($11,000 for 4 units), licensing (51 liquor licenses), tax collection (84% or $22 million collected at clerk's office counter), pet licensing (brought back in-house saving $54.95 previously paid to third party Pet Data), and the quarterly newsletter costing approximately $30,000 annually. Discussion included the pet license fee increase from $10 to $20 driven by Marathon County's increased fees to municipalities, and the $175 per animal cost for non-dog strays including mice. Chief election inspector pay may be increased from $13 to $15/hour for the next term.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well with Saturday operations starting at 6 AM. They have expanded to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors. Produce vendors with greenhouses are attending; full produce season expected mid to end of June. Vendors reported being very happy with the setup and location. Committee members raised concerns about parking congestion on Alderson and Newport streets.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and others, encouraging outreach to interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Clerk's office to continue seeking additional part-time staff member
Consider increasing chief election inspector pay to $15/hour for next two-year term
Recruit citizens to fill open committee spots
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, approving amendments to the nuisance ordinance for recommendation to the board, advancing an e-bike ordinance with added emoto restrictions, and authorizing a raze order for a condemned property at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also approved a mobile food vendor fire inspection program and conditionally approved a crosswalk on Alderson Street if Mount Olive Church covers the cost.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
Mobile food vendor fire inspection ordinance

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke critically about the new farmers market location, stating his opinion that 'it's a terrible execution' and predicting the park's grassy areas will be destroyed. He raised concerns about parking on grass potentially violating village code, ADA handicap parking placement on grass versus asphalt, the addition of fire inspections for mobile food vendors, and code enforcement cases that have been pending for extended periods without visible progress.

Code enforcement officer Audrey noted that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work summer zoning violations for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed the final copy of proposed nuisance ordinance amendments, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet). Motion by Olsen, seconded by Kimpie, to recommend approval to the board of trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred about the proposed e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion included whether electric scooters used by middle schoolers would be affected and officer discretion in enforcement. The committee decided to add language prohibiting emotos from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend approval with the emoto restriction passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson initiated discussion noting Weston's 12-hour parking restriction window (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most have 5-hour windows). Public Works Director Michael explained the even/odd system helps ensure plows can clear at least half the roadway. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight restrictions, noting it gives officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. The committee decided to table the item until July to allow Michael to identify specific problem areas that might need signing rather than village-wide restrictions.

Discussion on Mount Olive Church's request for a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest parking lot. Michael estimated a basic crosswalk would cost about $2,000, while a lighted system would be $25,000-30,000. Committee members expressed reluctance to pay for it, citing no accident history. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend board approval if Mount Olive agrees to cover the installation cost passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Mobile Food Vendor Fire Inspection Ordinance
discussions.body: Chief Hunt explained this creates a regional inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston, where one inspection with a sticker is valid across all municipalities. He noted Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Fire Marshal Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Kimpie, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Planning staff presented three address correction cases for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicowski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue needing even number assignment. Chief Hunt mentioned another issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicowski where an 8-plex has separate addresses for each unit. Motion passed unanimously to recommend board approval.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings for a condemned structure abandoned since 2010-2011. The fire department had previously declined to do a training burn due to unsafe conditions. Staff noted the current owners had housed chickens inside the structure. Demolition costs will be added to the property tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Kimpie, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt provided public comment criticizing the new farmers market location execution, raising concerns about parking on grass violating village code, ADA accessibility for handicap parking on grass, the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks, and lack of progress on long-standing code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance ordinance amendments to board of trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to board of trustees for approval
Public Works Director to identify specific areas needing parking signage for July meeting discussion
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing; if they agree to pay, recommend board approval
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for 5915 Delicowski, Ross Avenue properties, and 2211 Radkey Avenue recommended to board
Raze order authorized for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property concern at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
